specification.topic-statement: The viewport tracks how far the user has scrolled up from the bottom of the document and derives the visible row range used to clip layer rendering on each frame.

scope.in-scope: The scroll offset field, scroll commands and their step sizes, automatic scroll-to-bottom triggers, maximum offset calculation, viewport row derivation, and resize clamping.
scope.boundary: How the derived viewport row is used to clip and render individual layers is covered by the layer rendering specification.

The terminal row where the input buffer layer begins is calculated as part of viewport derivation and exposed to the cursor positioning code. The calculation is part of this specification; how the cursor uses that row is out of scope.

field.name: scroll offset
field.note: number of document rows the view has been scrolled up from the bottom; 0 means the bottom of the document is visible

field.name: scrollback start line
field.note: index of the first scrollback logical line visible in the current frame

field.name: scrollback line count
field.note: number of scrollback logical lines visible

field.name: input buffer start row
field.note: terminal row (0-indexed from top) at which the input buffer layer begins; equals the terminal height when the input buffer is off-screen

field.name: separator visible
field.note: true when the separator layer intersects the visible range

behavior.scroll-offset-semantics:
The scroll offset is a per-agent value representing how many document rows the view has been scrolled upward from the natural bottom position. An offset of zero means the bottom of the document is visible. An offset of N means the view has been scrolled up by N rows.
The offset is stored on each agent independently, so switching between agents preserves each agent's scroll position.
behavior.scroll-commands:
Four scroll commands modify the offset:
- **Scroll up** (mouse wheel up): advances the offset by 3 rows, clamped to the maximum offset. - **Scroll down** (mouse wheel down): reduces the offset by 3 rows, clamped to zero. - **Page up**: advances the offset by one full terminal height in rows, clamped to the maximum offset. - **Page down**: reduces the offset by one full terminal height in rows, clamped to zero.
behavior.auto-scroll-to-bottom:
The scroll offset is reset to zero (scroll to bottom) by any of the following:
- Any character typed into the input buffer. - Backspace, forward delete, or any line-kill key pressed. - Any cursor movement key (arrow keys, line-start, line-end). - The input buffer submitted (Enter pressed). - The LLM begins producing a response.
The effect is that any active user input or new LLM output snaps the view back to the bottom, ensuring the most recent content is always visible during interactive use.
behavior.maximum-offset-calculation:
The maximum valid offset is the document height minus the terminal height, clamped to zero when the document fits entirely within the terminal. The document height is the sum of the heights of all visible layers at the current terminal width.
Before calculating the maximum, layout for the scrollback buffer and the input buffer is updated to the current terminal width if it has changed.
behavior.viewport-derivation:
On each frame, the visible row range is derived from the scroll offset and terminal height:
- The last visible document row is the document height minus one minus the scroll offset. - The first visible document row is the last visible row plus one minus the terminal height. - These bounds define which layers and which rows within each layer are passed to the layer rendering pipeline.
The derived values (first visible row, terminal height) are set on the layer stack before rendering, so that each layer's render function receives pre- clipped row parameters.
When the terminal shrinks, the document height may decrease, making a previously valid offset larger than the new maximum. The offset is not corrected on the stored field; instead the clamped value is used when deriving the viewport on the next frame. The stored offset remains out-of-range until the next scroll command or auto-scroll event.
behavior.input-buffer-position-derivation:
The terminal row where the input buffer begins is calculated by accumulating the heights of all visible layers above the input buffer (banner, scrollback, spinner, separator) and subtracting the first visible document row. If the resulting position is negative or falls entirely below the terminal height, the input buffer is considered off-screen and its start row is reported as the terminal height.
If the input buffer starts above the viewport (the user has scrolled up so far that the top of the input buffer is above the first visible row), the start row is reported as zero (clamped to the top of the terminal).
